narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings—dreaming of law school only to have the door close can feel like a punch to the gut, and I'm truly sorry you're facing this right now. The admissions model looked at your profile and saw a 77% chance of struggling to pass the bar exam down the line, which tipped the scales against your application. What stood out most was your Year 3 decile of 3, meaning you ranked in the lower third of your class by then—that was the biggest signal to the model that challenges might persist into the high-stakes bar prep. Your Year 1 decile of 5 reinforced that, showing your grades started middling and trended downward from there. Layered on top was your LSAT score of 41, which the model viewed as another hurdle in competing with top performers on the exam. Coming from a middle-income family added to the picture, as it sometimes means fewer resources for the intense tutoring or prep that bar success often demands. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.3 fit into this pattern too, suggesting a consistent but not standout academic track record. Even factors like being a white male pursuing full-time studies nudged the prediction toward tougher odds, based on patterns the model has seen in past students. It's not that any one thing doomed you—it's how they all combined to paint a story of someone who might face an uphill battle against the bar's demands. This isn't the end of your path; many have turned similar profiles around with targeted prep or alternative routes into law. You've got real strengths here worth building on—what do you think your next step could be? Hang in there; you've already shown grit just by applying.
